review_title: A Promising Adaptation That Loses Its Way in the Corridors of Sentimentality
review_text: The announcement of a film adaptation of the viral indie game "Exit 8" was met with justified excitement. With over two million copies sold and a dedicated global fanbase, the project seemed destined for success. The film's acclaimed premiere at Cannes, where it received an eight-minute standing ovation and was hailed by some as the first truly successful video game adaptation, only heightened the anticipation. As a fan of the game's uniquely claustrophobic and paranoid atmosphere, I entered the cinema with high hopes for a masterclass in psychological horror. Unfortunately, director Genki Kawamura's interpretation, while technically proficient, ultimately betrays the source material's core premise.The first act is a masterful, faithful recreation of the game's eerie universe. The premise is simple: a man must escape an endless underground corridor by identifying the one "anomaly" among seemingly identical passages. Kawamura and cinematographer Akiko Ashizawa nail the aesthetic. The color grading is stark and unsettling, the camerawork-often employing long, unbroken takes-is immersive, and the sound design masterfully builds a sense of dread. You feel the protagonist's isolation and paranoia firsthand, scanning every inch of the frame for subtle, terrifying inconsistencies. Actor Kazunari Ninomiya is compelling in these early stages, delivering a physically convincing performance as an asthmatic man pushed to the brink of exhaustion and panic.The film's fatal flaw arrives in the form of a narrative addition not present in the game: a child. This is not merely an expansion of the lore; it is a complete genre shift. The tight focus on claustrophobia, existential absurdity, and subtle psychological terror is abruptly abandoned for a saccharine and wholly unnecessary subplot about rescue and redemption. The meticulously built atmosphere dissipates instantly, replaced by a sentimental drama that feels tonally alien and dramatically weak.What follows is a tedious second act that forgets it's supposed to be a horror-thriller. The tension flatlines as the plot meanders. The anomalies-the very heart of the game's terror-become sparse, static, and largely uninteresting, more of a background nuisance than a central threat. The potential for a rich tapestry of ever-escalating, creatively terrifying phenomena is squandered. For a concept ripe with possibilities for psychedelic horror and mind-bending suspense, the film offers a startling lack of variety and genuine scares. A single effective jump scare arrives far too late, by which point the audience's engagement has already waned.There is undeniable craft here. The production design is a perfect replica of the game's setting, and Yamato Koti is brilliantly unsettling as the "Walking Man." Kawamura, a veteran producer of works like "Your Name." and "A Silent Voice," clearly understands quality. However, his decision to prioritize emotional "depth" over visceral horror is a miscalculation that will leave most fans of the game deeply disappointed. It feels less like a passionate adaptation and more like a missed opportunity, a project rushed to capitalize on the game's fame without fully committing to its terrifying soul.The verdict is a frustrating one: "Exit 8" is a well-shot, well-acted film that fails as an adaptation. It might stand as a passable drama for viewers completely unfamiliar with the source material, but for those who cherished the game's oppressive atmosphere, it's a betrayal. One can only hope this paves the way for a future, more faithful remake-perhaps by Korean masters of tense, atmospheric horror like Kim Jee-woon or Yeon Sang-ho-that truly understands the terrifying genius of its original premise.
author: D0SKA

review_text: I watched The Exit 8 during the Fantasy Film Festival in Stuttgart and specifically chose this film because I had seen gameplay of the video game it's based on a few years ago. I was fascinated by its minimalist aesthetic and seemingly simple mechanics.The film largely follows the same premise as the game: our nameless protagonist finds himself trapped at the very beginning in what appears to be an endlessly repeating subway underpass - a single white, sterile corridor with a few small details here and there, and a mysterious, silent man.To escape the loop, the protagonist must keep walking through the tunnel, trying to reach the 8th level. However, so-called "anomalies" occasionally appear: lights begin to flicker, symbols or posters suddenly look different, or new objects appear where none were before. Whenever such an anomaly occurs, the rule is clear - turn back immediately!Unlike in the game, the film gives the main character a short backstory at the beginning, which might explain why he, of all people, is trapped in this loop. Because it's not only about escaping, but also about growing up and learning to take responsibility.What I especially liked about the film is how it involves the audience - you're encouraged to make decisions along the way. Is this an anomaly, or is it safe to move forward? It's very similar to the feeling of playing the game in first-person perspective as you guide your character through the labyrinth.The minimalist setting feels believable and, at times, reminiscent of Cube - the entire story takes place in a confined space, yet thanks to clever shifts in perspective, the premise holds up remarkably well over its 95-minute runtime.When some horror elements appear toward the end, they are barely visualized - never fully shown - forcing you to use your imagination, which, at least for me, created a genuine sense of unease. It's intelligent horror rather than cheap jump scares.Unfortunately, the characters, much like the setting, are rather simplistic, making it difficult to form a deeper emotional connection - they end up feeling somewhat interchangeable, and the performances don't particularly stand out.Another point of criticism: early in the film, it's established that our protagonist suffers from asthma and runs out of his inhaler, which initially adds a sense of urgency to his need to escape the loop. However, this element is never revisited later in the story and feels somewhat forgotten.Something else that bothered me - and that I've noticed in other East Asian productions as well - is that the protagonist, especially in the film's early scenes, tends to verbalize everything he sees, even when it's obvious to the audience. Because this feels unnatural, it constantly reminded me of my role as a viewer and made it harder to fully immerse myself in those moments.In summary, The Exit 8 is a gripping, minimalist psychological thriller that dares, for once, not to explain how its situation came to be. Through smart perspective changes, the film successfully expands a simple premise into a feature-length narrative and keeps the viewer actively engaged. What's missing, however, is a stronger emotional connection to the characters.

author: 0U

review_title: An Intriguing Concept That Struggles to Fully Translate
review_text: "Exit 8" is a film that immediately sparked curiosity, especially for those familiar with the original game and its uniquely unsettling premise. The source material was memorable for its simplicity, atmosphere, and quiet tension - so seeing it adapted into a feature film is both surprising and ambitious. While the result isn't bad, it ultimately feels a bit underwhelming.The movie does a decent job of preserving the eerie tone and minimalist feel that made the game so intriguing. There's an effort to maintain that sense of unease and repetition, and visually, it captures the sterile, liminal atmosphere fairly well. You can clearly see the respect for the original concept.That said, the transition from game to film proves challenging. What works brilliantly in an interactive format doesn't always translate into a compelling narrative structure. The film struggles to expand the premise in a way that feels fully engaging, and as a result, some sections feel repetitive or stretched thin rather than tense and immersive.Performances are serviceable, but the characters never become a strong emotional anchor. This isn't entirely surprising given the nature of the source material, yet it does limit the film's impact. The pacing is uneven at times, wavering between moments of intrigue and stretches that feel less inspired.In the end, "Exit 8" is a respectable attempt at adapting a very unconventional game. It's watchable and occasionally effective, but it never quite reaches the level of tension or originality that made the game stand out. Not bad, not great - an interesting experiment that falls a bit short of its potential.
author: Hakihiko

review_text: Exit 8 is extremely frustrating. It's weird for the sake of being weird, goes around in endless loops, and never delivers a proper ending. The whole movie feels like you're stuck watching the same thing over and over with no real payoff. If you don't already know the game it's based on, there's really no point to this film-it makes little sense, explains nothing, and doesn't offer the viewer any closure. Instead of building tension or meaning, it just drags you in circles until you're left wondering why you invested your time. Confusing, empty, and ultimately a waste of time.
author: eldalatijana

review_title: a film that's both moving and provoking
review_text: The story of the film pulled me in immediately it had a raw depth that lingered long after the credits. I loved the tension and anger I felt when certain details were overlooked. That frustration showed me how invested I had become. The emotions portrayed were striking and authentic, giving the film a powerful edge.What makes it fascinating is the paradox: it feels so deep in its themes, yet at times superficial in its execution almost as if it left space for us to fill in the blanks with our own interpretations.At its core, the film poses a timeless question: if life handed us chances, would we truly take them, or would we wait, hoping for a clearer sign? That lingering thought makes the movie unforgettable, even beyond its imperfections.

author: JanC-655

review_title: The Best Game-to-Film Horror That Subjects the Male Psyche to Sterile Terror
review_text: Men, don't worry - you get your moment in cinema too.Male suffering is rarely the focus in horror-at least not without punishment, ridicule, or monstrosity attached. "Exit 8" shifts that. It offers a vision of masculine fear that's quieter, more existential, and no less devastating."Exit 8" is a 2025 Japanese psychological horror film written and directed by Genki Kawamura-known for emotionally resonant dramas like "If Cats Disappeared from the World"-makes a surprising but confident leap into mind-bending terror, based on the 2023 indie video game The Exit 8, developed by Kotake Create.If you've heard of or played the game, you'll know the premise: A man is trapped in an endless, sterile subway passageway. His goal? Find Exit 8.The rules are deceptively simple: Don't overlook anything out of the ordinary. If you spot an anomaly, turn back immediately. If not, keep moving. Only then will you escape through Exit 8.But one wrong move, one missed clue, and you're back to the beginning.The film sticks to this eerie structure and runs a lean 95 minutes, testing your nerves as much as the protagonist's. You're not just watching...you're playing along, scanning each frame for the uncanny, the out-of-place, the abnormal.The setting-a cold, fluorescent-lit corridor that feels clinically infinite-becomes a character in itself.This isn't gore-soaked horror. It's psychological terror-the kind that seeps into your bones.Paranoia escalates in a relentless loop, echoing cult classics like "Cube," "Run Lola Run," or "Dead End." The idea of escape seems less like a possibility and more like a fading memory. But...While it's clearly a game adaptation, "Exit 8"rises above expectations. It's easily one of the best video game-to-film translations I've seen.That said, making a bad film out of this concept would've almost required a deliberate effort-the source material is already packed with atmosphere.Yet Kawamura doesn't just adapt-he expands.In the tradition of films like "Pulse" and "Cure," "Exit 8" favors mood and psychological disintegration over jump scares or gore.The film dives deep into the grind of modern Japanese urban life-and by extension, modern life everywhere. Wake up. Get dressed. Commute. Work. Return home. Face societal expectations to settle down, have children, build the dream.But what if that dream isn't yours? What if you're stuck in someone else's loop?What makes "Exit 8" especially compelling is how the endless underground corridor seems to represent not just a physical trap, but a psychological one-the inner landscape of a man torn between duty, expectation, and personal truth.It's worth noting that the film opens with a cryptic phone call from his girlfriend, the true meaning of which slowly emerges as the story unfolds.These themes have often been explored through female narratives-which are vital-but rarely are men allowed to be vulnerable protagonists in this kind of psychological horror.Men, too, are bound by expectations that can quietly unmake them.As for the filmmaking itself-it's stellar. The cinematography and production design conjure a surreal, almost dreamlike atmosphere from the most mundane of settings. It's a haunting visual paradox.The performances are quietly captivating, with Kazunari Ninomiya delivering a nuanced portrayal that balances desperation with quiet resolve. Yuki Kochi, in a supporting role, adds an unpredictable energy that keeps you on edge.In the end, "Exit 8" is a devilishly engrossing piece of existential dread horror. It's smart, stylish, and unsettling-culminating in a finale that forces both the Lost Man and the viewer to ask:Why leave? And what are you really escaping from?

author: Dwayney-5

review_title: Psychological Trope Movies Are So Overdone. BORING.
review_text: Let me be clear, the only reason why I'm giving it stars at all is because of Kazunari Ninomiya. That man is a gem and forever will be.That being said, this movie is annoying. It leans on the "representational/psychological" angle too heavily. Which gives the director or writers a blank check for weirdness instead of a structured mystery. The original game had really tight rules: spot anomalies - survive, miss them - restart. That clarity gave players a sense of logic even inside the surreal. The movie loosens that up and smears it with symbolism, so you don't get a definitive payoff. Just vibes and "interpret it yourself." WHICH IS OVERDONE AND SOOOOO SO LAZY.That's why the ending feels cheap. Instead of answering why the loop exists, it shifts the burden to the viewer's headspace ("it's your psyche," "it's trauma," "it's purgatory"). Which is an easy out compared to committing to either a supernatural law or a sci-fi anomaly and sticking with it. Like why is it so hard for writers these days to actually stick to a plot is it really that much harder than the psychological warzone trope?????

author: lolthisiskohncena

review_text: I'm surprised that I was genuinely invested in the story from the start and completely blown away by how impressive it is. You know why? Okay, so this movie is based on the 2023 Japanese horror game of the same name, and there's no actual plot in the game itself; you just have to find anomalies to escape this never-ending passageway. I mean think about it, how one could make a movie based on a video game with no real narrative and not even a character to be developed in the first place?. Well, director Genki Kawamura and co-writer Kentaro Hirase obviously took what's already been in the game, and they expanded it and deepened the mystery very brilliantly. Knowing how the gameplay is, you'll probably expect this to be a long, tedious movie. I kid you not, I didn't find myself in a position where I was about to be bored as hell. Also, what's great about the script is that they took us back and forth to three different characters' POV in this hellhole, which makes the whole experience even more exciting. This movie is so good at evoking the feeling of frustration and despair in the act of possibly the worst situation a person could be in.Not mentioning Exit 8' undoubtedly excellent technical feats would be downplaying its strongest aspect. It's obvious that they don't have a big amount of budget, and some scenes definitely show, but honestly, I was baffled by how they approached it. The close-ups and tracking shots really put us there to be in the moment with these people, the use of Steadicam and seamless editing heighten the immersion. Exit 8 has a "videogame adaptation" written all over it, essentially capturing basically everything that the source material has to a great extent.

review_title: A Disappointing Adaptation That Misses the Mark Entirely
review_text: As a dedicated fan of the game, I had high expectations for this adaptation, expectations that were shattered within the first 30 minutes. I couldn't bring myself to continue watching what can only be described as a complete misfire.The opening sequence is unnecessarily messy and sets a chaotic tone that never recovers. What should have been an engaging introduction instead feels disjointed and amateurish. The constant barrage of heavy breathing, exaggerated sound effects, and intrusive noises becomes grating almost immediately, turning what could have been atmospheric tension into an irritating distraction.The acting is equally problematic. Performances feel forced and unnatural, lacking the depth and nuance that made the game's characters compelling. It's as if the cast was directed to overact every scene, resulting in moments that feel more comedic than dramatic... and not in a good way.Most frustratingly, this film completely abandons the psychological horror elements that made the source material so effective. Despite being labelled as horror and psychological thriller on IMDb, there's nothing genuinely unsettling or thought-provoking here. It's devoid of the atmospheric dread and intelligent storytelling that fans of the game loved.My honest advice: skip this movie entirely and play (or replay) the game instead. The game delivers everything this adaptation promises but fails to achieve. This film serves no purpose and adds nothing of value to the franchise. It's a cash-grab that never needed to exist.Save your time and revisit the source material, you'll thank yourself.

review_text: I don't normally watch horror films but this time I made an exception, unwittingly though, but I don't regret it. Exit 8, based on an eponymous video game, is a new social horror from Japan, set in a subway passage, where the main character gets stuck in a loop after getting off the train and talking to his ex-girlfriend on the phone.The film offers an engaging way of criticizing the modern Japanese (and maybe international) society where each individual is focused on themselves, blind to the outside world, incapable of facing emotional challenges and reluctant to get out of their own loop.

review_title: Great movie, but not for everyone
review_text: This is a great psychological thriller, with heavy emphasis on psychological.However, this movie is not for everyone.This is an adaptation of the video game Exit 8, and it does a very well job of giving you that eerie feeling of walking through the underground with the use of sound (which is one of the movie's stronger points) and minimal cuts in footage to really drive though that feeling of authenticity and realism.There are a lot of hidden messages and metaphors used as a way to comment on different subjects, but also tell a story.Do not expect this movie to hold your hand, because it will not. A lot if left up to your own interpretation, which is why i think this movie shines and works so well. Instead of a clear cut objective or a ghost you have to vanquish, there is no clear goal here, except for of course: "Escape".The movie touches on subjects such as parenthood, Japanese work and public transport culture and abortion.If you are not necessarily into these kinds of metaphors or movies attempts to question norms in culture or trying to convey messages, it might not be for you.Although i have to say to give this movie a chance, especially in the theatres. Unless you have a complete cinema experience at home, i highly recommend giving it a try in the theatres. The sound is really one of its strong suits!

author: AntonY-424

review_text: Exit 8 takes a premise so spare it sounds like a dare-walk a sterile corridor, notice what's wrong, decide whether to press on and turns it into an unnervingly immersive experience. What could have been a gimmick becomes a study in attention: the movie treats observation as action, asking you to scan every frame for tiny ruptures in reality and making your success (or failure) feel personal. It's the kind of low-budget ingenuity that recalls Cube: a handful of sets, a strict ruleset, and a filmmaker confident enough to let repetition do the heavy lifting.The filmmaking leans into a "single-take" feel without becoming a stunt. Long, unbroken passages keep you trapped in the same geometry as the protagonist, while quiet shifts in framing, prop placement, and blocking supply the variation. The soundscape matters as much as the image fluorescents buzz, ventilation wheezes, footsteps measure out dread so that each new pass down the hallway registers like a loaded coin flip. The puzzles aren't just for the character; they recruit the viewer. You're not waiting for a jump scare so much as policing the frame, wondering whether a sign just moved a few centimeters or a poster's font is microscopically different.That design choice creates the film's biggest strength and its lone real risk. The cadence is intentionally steady, even stubborn, and stretches in the middle flirt with sameness. For some, the loop will feel hypnotic; for others, it may read as wheel-spinning. But the pattern does escalate-quietly, precisely-and when the film cashes in its small, cumulative dislocations, the payoff lands with the clean click of a solved puzzle.The performances sell the spiral. With so little room for conventional set pieces, micro-reactions do the work: a breath held half a beat too long, eyes tracking a corner that shouldn't be interesting, the flicker of "did I miss it?" across a face. It's disciplined, ego-free acting that keeps a formal exercise human.In the end, Exit 8 is proudly small and unexpectedly deep: a minimalist corridor that opens into a maze of paranoia, a ruleset that doubles as narrative engine. If you enjoy puzzle-box storytelling and films that reward vigilance over volume, this one's a sly thrill. The pacing won't charm everyone, but the craft is sharp, the concept is tight, and the execution rarely slips.

review_text: No pun intended - the number 8 stands for what? Well I reckon you can guess that on your own. But how do you make a movie interesting that more or less does the same thing over and over again? Well you have to rely on your actors but also on your script.Also it doesn't hurt to make different sections ... at least story wise. What can we do different? Well the movie itself is telling you that to progress you have to take things into your own hands ... at least that is what I am reading into it. Smaller issues (flaws?) aside (time and line to name one, if you remember the time ... not Michael Jackson style) ... this is really well thought of.Suspend your disbelief and go with the flow - sometimes you have to go back to move forward ... so many messages to read into it ... if you care to see and read them.

review_text: I don't know if this is adapted from a game, but for me, it's a masterpiece.This movie might not be for everyone. author: arekelek

review_title: Only works because the characters are insanely stupid
review_text: I liked the minimalistic concept.It is competently filmed, with decent production quality.I really enjoyed the movie until about the half point. And there's a reason : This is the kind of story that would work very well in a short film or in a TV show episode. It can't hold a 90mn run-time (with credits), which is already the minimum.So, to increase artificially the length of the movie, they have the characters make insanely dumb decisions, every single time. And I mean, really really dumb. The kind of movie where you yell at your screen, because they keep making the same dumb mistakes, again and again and again. In the end, I was more frustrated than satisfied.

review_text: My favourite genre but ruined by making the protagonist have breathing problems. I couldnt continue watching someone struggle breathing. Its like torture and i dont think its intentional... it doesnt add anything to the story. And the guy using his asthma spray wrong... ruined the whole movie.And its such a pity, it looked totally in my alley. The poor guy is trapped already, why did you have to make him miserable too?!
author: apo_cello

review_title: About as meh as the game
review_text: I will be honest here.. I dove into this movie blind and when i noticed (about 13 minutes in is when it becomes clear) they made it after an adult video game, i was distraught. You see, i have said video game and said video game is a huge pile of hot steaming manure. I had a moment of panic as to how bad this movie was going to be.Fortunately they strayed quite far from the game and built up the movie around its own ideas and cinematography. Outside the general concept of the game and its instructions to the player, this movie is not tied to it, which is a good thing!Unfortunately, the movie creates its own faults and convoluted aimless scenes. It also commits the cardinal sin of the horror genre: having kids in it for no reason.Up until the mid point it manages to maintain some suspense and feeling of despair. Afterwards tho it just runs out of idea and starts throwing in random stuff, including mind teleportation scenes, imaginary memories and all sorts of junk to fill up the script. They missed a lot of opportunities to really liven up the plot. No artistic freedom was used here which i would assume is because it's budget felt like 1000 yen...The ending.. seriously. Discount "inception"... we must loop deeper, extend the loop, loop it all. So there is no ending to speak of. Unrewarding, unmemorable, unfulfilling, forgettable.4\10 the movie doesnt fall far from the game.
author: idonotexist

review_text: Two men and a boy wander a subway corridor tiled in white, possibly looking for an exit. Occasionally something weird happens, such as a brown flood, a power outage, an infant squalling in a storage locker, or the escape of a group of sewer rats, each mutated by a giant piece of a human face. That's an hour and a half I'll never get back. Garbled inanity.
author: ebaymonger

review_title: Faithful to the Game, But I Wanted More Fear
review_text: Exit 8 is a solid adaptation that clearly respects its source material, and that's both its biggest strength and its main limitation. If you've played the game, you'll immediately recognize the atmosphere - the endless underground passage, the quiet tension, the obsessive focus on small details. The movie captures that loop-like feeling really well, and visually it's pretty spot on.What I appreciated most is how committed it is to the game's rules and tone. It doesn't try to over-explain things or turn it into a loud, action-heavy horror film. The repetition, the subtle changes in the environment, and the constant doubt about whether something is "wrong" are all handled with care. It's slow, restrained, and intentionally minimal, which will definitely work for fans of the game.That said, as someone watching it purely as a movie, I couldn't help but wish it pushed the horror further. The tension is there, but it rarely crosses into genuinely scary territory. It's more unsettling than frightening. There were moments where I expected a stronger payoff - something darker, more disturbing - but the film stays very controlled and almost too polite with its scares.The pacing might also test some viewers' patience. The repetition is thematically important, but there are stretches where it feels like the movie is relying a bit too much on the concept alone. I never found it boring, but I did feel like it was holding back when it could've gone harder.Overall, Exit 8 is a respectful, well-made adaptation that nails the vibe of the game, even if it doesn't fully take advantage of the horror potential. I enjoyed it, I just wanted to be more scared. A comfortable 7/10 - especially if you're already familiar with the game.
author: ahmetcanerkara1

review_title: A solid atmosphere ruined by a protagonist who moves like a tank
review_text: As an adaptation of the viral game, Exit 8 does a fantastic job of capturing the pure dread and desperation of being trapped in an endless, clinical subway labyrinth. The tension is thick, and you can really feel the character's growing hopelessness as he tries to find a way out. Visually and atmospherically, it's a very solid thriller.However, the film suffers from a major pacing issue that stems from the character's behavior. The way he investigates anomalies is incredibly tedious. In a real life-or-death situation, you'd be quickly glancing left and right to spot changes. Instead, the protagonist turns his entire body and head in slow, sweeping motions like he's stuck in a clunky game engine. It makes the "investigation" parts feel boring rather than tense.Because of this slow buildup, the climax-when he finally meets another character-doesn't hit as hard as it should. It loses its impact because the audience is already a bit drained from the repetitive movements.The movie also fails to communicate the character's mental state effectively. Sure, we can miss an anomaly, but we aren't stupid enough to walk straight into an obvious trap right before reaching Exit 8. If the point was to show the character losing his sanity, the film didn't deliver that intensity. It just made the character look incompetent rather than mentally exhausted.Verdict: It's a great concept with a very strong "liminal space" vibe. If you liked the game, you'll enjoy seeing it on screen, but the sluggish character movement and lack of psychological depth keep it from being a masterpiece.
